holdings.reason: Down 26% from 52-week high despite 85% YoY revenue growth and Data Center +92% — Broadcom sector contagion created a real fundamental dislocation in the strongest AI infra name

The Great Rotation is structural: Fed rate cuts from late 2025 are NOW filtering into small cap floating-rate balance sheets (12-18mo lag). Retail sales beat today confirms domestic economy holds. This is the right entry window.

thesis: Two distinct themes worth owning here. First, NVDA is a fundamental dislocation — its own revenue is up 85% YoY yet it's 26% below its 52-week high because of Broadcom's weaker AI guide, which is Broadcom's problem, not NVDA's. Second, the small-cap rotation (IWM) is structural and timely: the Fed's 175bps of cuts from late 2025 take 12-18 months to reduce floating-rate interest expense on small cap balance sheets, and May-July 2026 is precisely that window. Retail sales today came in flat vs. -0.4% expected, supporting the domestic economy case. Deploying 30%, keeping 70% cash ahead of Warsh's first FOMC press conference June 16-17 — his hawkish reputation and the April 8-4 split vote make this a genuine wildcard.
daily_note: Day 1: Initiated NVDA and IWM. NVDA's Broadcom-induced selloff is sector contagion, not a NVDA problem — 85% revenue growth and 26% below the high is a real entry. IWM's rate-cut tailwind is hitting balance sheets NOW, exactly when seasonal small-cap strength peaks. Sitting 70% cash for Warsh.
outlook: Watching Warsh's June 16-17 FOMC press conference and dot plot above everything else. Hawkish surprise (cuts pushed to 2027) would pressure growth and give better entries on NVDA and QQQ — that's the plan. Soft tone or dovish revision = add IWM and consider SMH. Also monitoring SPCX post-IPO support levels (not chasing at $167, watching for a base) and AMD's Meta 6GW GPU deal execution as a read on enterprise AI compute demand.
tweet: Day 1 live. $NVDA down 26% from highs with 85% revenue growth — that's Broadcom's problem, not theirs. $IWM small cap rotation has a structural tailwind: rate cut savings hitting balance sheets RIGHT NOW. 70% cash through Warsh's FOMC debut. Two real theses, not FOMO.
